title: Advanced Input Pipeline
description: The complete input system from hardware interrupt to View.onTouchEvent: kernel, InputFlinger, IPC, and dispatch
lesson:
Section 1: Hardware to Kernel

Every touch begins as an electrical signal. The touch controller IC on the display digitizer detects capacitance changes when your finger approaches the screen. This fires a hardware interrupt (IRQ) to the ARM processor.

Finger touches screen │ ▼ Touch Controller IC (e.g., Synaptics, Goodix, FocalTech) │ Detects capacitance change → fires hardware IRQ ▼ Linux Kernel Input Subsystem │ IRQ handler → input driver → input_event() ▼ /dev/input/eventX (character device file) │ Each input device gets its own eventX node ▼ Userspace can read raw events via read(fd, ...)

The Linux kernel input subsystem is a generic framework for all human input devices. Input drivers register with the subsystem and produce events through /dev/input/eventX device files.

Input Event Types:
EV_ABS — Absolute position events (touchscreen: X, Y, pressure, contact area)
EV_KEY — Key press/release events (hardware buttons, keyboard keys, power button)
EV_REL — Relative movement events (trackpad, mouse, scroll wheel)
EV_SYN — Synchronization events (marks the end of a batch of events from one report)

Each raw event is a simple struct: {timestamp, type, code, value}. For example, a touch down produces a sequence like: EV_ABS/ABS_MT_TRACKING_ID=45, EV_ABS/ABS_MT_POSITION_X=540, EV_ABS/ABS_MT_POSITION_Y=960, EV_ABS/ABS_MT_PRESSURE=128, then EV_SYN to flush.

EventHub is Android's native C++ class that opens and monitors all /dev/input/* device files using epoll. It is the very first Android-specific code in the input pipeline. EventHub discovers input devices at boot, watches for hotplug via inotify, and reads raw input_event structs from the kernel.

Section 2: InputFlinger — The Native Input Service

InputFlinger is a native Binder service that runs in its own process, just like SurfaceFlinger and AudioFlinger. These are the "flinger" family — Android's performance-critical native services.

ServiceProcessJobWhy Native C++?
SurfaceFlingersurfaceflingerComposites all window layers into the final display frame60/90/120Hz frame deadlines
InputFlingerinputflinger (or in surfaceflinger)Reads input events, routes them to the correct window240Hz+ touch sample rates, sub-ms latency
AudioFlingeraudioserverMixes audio streams, routes to HALReal-time audio deadlines (~5ms buffer)
Why native C++ and not Java? These services are latency-critical. A Java service in system_server would suffer from GC pauses (unpredictable 5-50ms stalls), JNI overhead, and higher memory footprint. Touch sampling at 240Hz means each sample arrives every ~4.2ms — a single GC pause could drop multiple samples. Native C++ gives deterministic timing with no garbage collector.

On top of InputFlinger sits InputManagerService (IMS), a Java service running in system_server. IMS is the policy layer — it handles things like key mapping tables, input device configuration, input method dispatch policy, and pointer speed settings. IMS communicates with the native InputFlinger through a JNI bridge called NativeInputManager.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ system_server (Java) │ │ │ │ InputManagerService (IMS) │ │ ├── Policy decisions (key mapping, pointer │ │ │ speed, input method routing) │ │ ├── Receives callbacks from native layer │ │ └── Talks to WMS for window/focus info │ │ │ │ │ │ JNI (NativeInputManager) │ │ ▼ │ │ ┌──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ │ │ Native InputFlinger │ │ │ │ ├── InputReader thread │ │ │ │ ├── InputDispatcher thread │ │ │ │ └── EventHub │ │ │ └──────────────────────────────────────┘ │ └─────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Section 3: InputReader Thread

InputReader runs in its own dedicated native thread, separate from InputDispatcher. Its job is to transform raw kernel events into high-level Android events.

InputReader Thread (native, runs in a loop) │ ├── 1. Calls EventHub.getEvents() │ └── epoll_wait() on /dev/input/* fds │ └── Returns batch of raw input_event structs │ ├── 2. Device-specific processing │ ├── Touch calibration (raw coords → display coords) │ ├── Gesture detection (tap, swipe, pinch) │ ├── Palm rejection (large contact area = palm, not finger) │ ├── Multi-touch slot tracking (Linux MT protocol B) │ └── Stylus pressure/tilt processing │ ├── 3. Converts to Android event types │ ├── Raw EV_ABS → MotionEvent (ACTION_DOWN, MOVE, UP) │ ├── Raw EV_KEY → KeyEvent (ACTION_DOWN, ACTION_UP) │ └── Applies pointer speed, orientation transforms │ └── 4. Passes to InputDispatcher └── InputDispatcher.notifyMotion() └── InputDispatcher.notifyKey()

The key transformation here is coordinate mapping. The touch controller reports in its own coordinate space (e.g., 0-4095 for a 12-bit digitizer). InputReader maps these to display pixels, applying rotation transforms (portrait vs landscape) and any calibration offsets from /system/usr/idc/*.idc input device configuration files.

Section 4: InputDispatcher Thread

InputDispatcher is the routing brain of the input system. It runs in its own native thread and determines which window should receive each event.

InputDispatcher Thread │ ├── Receives processed events from InputReader │ ├── For TOUCH events: Hit Testing │ ├── Gets window list from WindowManagerService │ ├── Windows are ordered by Z-order (top to bottom) │ ├── For coordinates (x, y), finds the topmost visible │ │ window whose bounds contain the point │ ├── Considers: window visibility, touchable regions, │ │ FLAG_NOT_TOUCHABLE, FLAG_NOT_FOCUSABLE │ └── Result: InputTarget (window handle + coordinate transform) │ ├── For KEY events: Focus-based routing │ ├── Gets focused window from WindowManagerService │ └── Keys always go to the focused window │ ├── Resolves the InputTarget │ ├── Contains: InputChannel (socket fd), window transform │ ├── Transforms coordinates from display space to window space │ └── Applies window scaling, offset, rotation │ └── Writes event to the target window's InputChannel (socket) └── If window is unresponsive → ANR detection kicks in
ANR Detection lives here. InputDispatcher tracks every dispatched event per window. If a window has not finished processing the previous event within the timeout, the system triggers an ANR:

Input event timeout: 5 seconds — if a window does not acknowledge a dispatched input event (touch/key) within 5 seconds.
BroadcastReceiver timeout: 10 seconds — for foreground broadcast receivers (background is 60s).

The dispatcher sets a timer when it sends an event. When the app finishes processing, it sends an acknowledgment back through the socket. If the timer fires before the ack, InputDispatcher calls into InputManagerService to report the ANR.

Section 5: The IPC — InputChannel (Unix Socket Pair)

This is where events cross the process boundary from system_server to the app. Android uses a Unix domain socket pair — not Binder — for input event delivery.

Why NOT Binder for input events?

Binder transactions go through the kernel Binder driver: the sender marshals data into a Parcel, calls ioctl() into the Binder driver, the driver copies data to the target process, the target unmarshals the Parcel, processes the call, marshals a reply, and the driver copies it back. This involves two context switches and two data copies minimum.

Unix sockets are direct kernel-buffered pipes: write(fd) copies data into a kernel buffer, the target process read(fd) copies it out. One context switch, one copy, no marshalling overhead. For events arriving at 240Hz (every ~4.2ms), the difference is critical.

The socket pair is created during window addition:

// 1. App inflates its layout and calls setView() ViewRootImpl.setView(decorView, layoutParams, ...) // 2. setView() calls into WindowManagerService via Binder mWindowSession.addToDisplayAsUser(mWindow, ...) // 3. WMS creates a socket pair // socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_SEQPACKET, 0, fds) // Server fd → stays in system_server (InputDispatcher) // Client fd → returned to app process via Binder InputChannel serverChannel = new InputChannel(); // server side InputChannel clientChannel = new InputChannel(); // client side InputChannel.openInputChannelPair(name, serverChannel, clientChannel); // 4. Server channel registered with InputDispatcher // 5. Client channel returned to app's ViewRootImpl
Window Registration and Socket Creation: App Process system_server ────────── ───────────── ViewRootImpl.setView() │ │ Binder call: WMS.addWindow() │─────────────────────────────────▶│ │ │ Creates socketpair() │ │ ┌─────────────────┐ │ │ │ server fd (s) │ │ │ │ client fd (c) │ │ │ └─────────────────┘ │ │ │ │ Registers server fd │ │ with InputDispatcher │ │ │◀── returns client fd ────────────│ │ │ Registers client fd with │ app's Looper via Looper.addFd() │ (epoll underneath) │ ▼ When data arrives on socket: epoll_wait() wakes the Looper │ ▼ WindowInputEventReceiver.onInputEvent() Event Flow (steady state): InputDispatcher App Looper ──────────────── ───────── │ │ │ write(server_fd, event) │ │──────────── socket ──────────────▶ │ │ kernel buffer │ epoll_wait() wakes │ │ read(client_fd, event) │ │ → onInputEvent() │ │ ... dispatches to Views ... │ │ │◀─── write(client_fd, finished) ───│ │ acknowledgment ("consumed") │ │ │ │ Clears ANR timer for this window │

Section 6: App-Side Event Delivery

Once the event arrives at the app process, it does NOT go directly to your Views. It passes through a pipeline of InputStages inside ViewRootImpl. This is a critical implementation detail that most Android developers never encounter.

// ViewRootImpl creates this pipeline in setView(): // Each stage gets a chance to handle the event before the next // Stage 1: NativePreImeInputStage // → Native key event pre-processing before IME // Stage 2: ViewPreImeInputStage // → View.dispatchKeyEventPreIme() — app can intercept // keys BEFORE the soft keyboard sees them // Stage 3: ImeInputStage // → Routes key events to InputMethodManager // → This is HOW the soft keyboard works: // key events go to IME first, IME can consume them // to produce composed text (e.g., multi-key CJK input) // Stage 4: EarlyPostImeInputStage // → Handles keyboard shortcuts, back button, etc. // Stage 5: NativePostImeInputStage // → Native post-IME processing // Stage 6: ViewPostImeInputStage ← TOUCH EVENTS ENTER HERE // → Calls mView.dispatchPointerEvent() // → This is where touch events enter the View tree
Why does this pipeline exist?

The soft keyboard (IME) needs first dibs on key events. When you type on a soft keyboard in a text field, the key events don't go to the View — they go to the InputMethodManager, which routes them to the IME process. The IME composes the text (handling things like autocomplete, CJK character composition, swipe typing) and then sends the composed text back to the View via InputConnection.

Touch events skip the IME stages entirely and go directly to ViewPostImeInputStage. But they still flow through the pipeline infrastructure.

Section 7: The View Dispatch Chain — Why Activity -> Window -> DecorView?

This is the part that confuses most developers. When a touch event reaches ViewPostImeInputStage, it doesn't go directly to your View. It takes a roundabout path through Activity, and there are very good reasons for this.

The Complete Touch Dispatch Chain: ViewPostImeInputStage │ │ calls mView.dispatchPointerEvent() │ (mView IS the DecorView) ▼ DecorView.dispatchTouchEvent() │ │ calls window.getCallback().dispatchTouchEvent() │ (the callback IS the Activity) ▼ Activity.dispatchTouchEvent() ◄── WHY HERE? │ ├── Calls onUserInteraction() on EVERY touch │ │ (resets screen timeout timer) │ ├── Activity can intercept/override globally │ └── Activity.onTouchEvent() is the fallback │ │ calls getWindow().superDispatchTouchEvent() ▼ PhoneWindow.superDispatchTouchEvent() │ │ calls mDecor.superDispatchTouchEvent() ▼ DecorView.superDispatchTouchEvent() │ │ calls super.dispatchTouchEvent() (ViewGroup) ▼ ViewGroup.dispatchTouchEvent() ◄── Hit testing starts │ ├── Calls onInterceptTouchEvent() │ └── Can return true to STEAL events from children │ (ScrollView, ViewPager do this on drag) │ ├── Iterates children in reverse drawing order (top-first) │ └── For each child: is (x,y) within child bounds? │ └── Yes → child.dispatchTouchEvent() │ └── If no child consumed → calls own onTouchEvent() ▼ View.dispatchTouchEvent() │ ├── OnTouchListener.onTouch() (if set) │ └── Returns true? → consumed, stop here │ └── onTouchEvent() ├── Handles click detection (DOWN + UP in bounds) ├── Handles long press detection (DOWN held > 500ms) └── Returns true (consumed) or false (not handled)
Why does DecorView route through Activity?

1. Activity.onUserInteraction() — Called on every user touch. The framework uses this to reset the screen timeout timer. Without this, the screen would dim/lock even while you're actively interacting with the app. It's also used for analytics and idle detection.

2. Global interception — Activity can override dispatchTouchEvent() to intercept all touches before any View sees them (e.g., lock screen overlays, tutorial overlays).

3. Fallback handling — If no View in the entire tree consumes the event, Activity.onTouchEvent() is called as the last resort. This is where you'd handle touches on the window background.
// The return value chain (consumed = true/false) // View.onTouchEvent() returns: // true → "I handled it, event consumed" // false → "Not my problem, pass it up" // ViewGroup.dispatchTouchEvent() returns: // true → a child (or self) consumed the event // false → nobody consumed it, goes up to parent // Activity.dispatchTouchEvent() returns: // true → consumed (by View tree or Activity.onTouchEvent) // false → not consumed (rare, since Activity is last resort) // CRITICAL for ACTION_DOWN: // If a View returns false for ACTION_DOWN, it will NOT receive // any subsequent MOVE or UP events for that gesture. // The framework remembers who consumed DOWN and sends the // rest of the gesture stream only to that View.

Section 8: Touch Event Flow Details

A touch gesture is a stream of events from ACTION_DOWN to ACTION_UP. Understanding this stream is essential for custom touch handling.

ActionMeaningWhen
ACTION_DOWNFirst finger touches screenStarts a gesture. The View that consumes this gets the entire event stream.
ACTION_MOVEFinger movedContinuous. Sent to the View that consumed ACTION_DOWN.
ACTION_UPLast finger liftedEnds the gesture. Click detection happens here (DOWN + UP in bounds).
ACTION_CANCELGesture cancelledParent called onInterceptTouchEvent() and returned true — parent stole the event.
ACTION_POINTER_DOWNAdditional finger touchesSecond, third, etc. finger in a multi-touch gesture.
ACTION_POINTER_UPAdditional finger liftsA non-primary finger lifts. The gesture continues with remaining fingers.
ACTION_CANCEL — The Steal Event:

When a parent's onInterceptTouchEvent() returns true, the child currently handling the gesture receives ACTION_CANCEL. This tells the child: "Stop what you're doing, clean up your state, your gesture was stolen." All subsequent events in this gesture go to the intercepting parent instead.

Classic example: You tap a button inside a ScrollView. The button receives ACTION_DOWN. You start dragging your finger. ScrollView's onInterceptTouchEvent() detects the drag and returns true. The button receives ACTION_CANCEL (and should un-highlight itself). ScrollView now receives all subsequent MOVE/UP events to perform scrolling.

MotionEvent Batching

Touch hardware reports at high frequency (240Hz+), but the app's main thread processes at the display frame rate (60/90/120Hz). To avoid flooding the message queue, Android batches multiple MOVE events into a single MotionEvent:

// A batched MotionEvent contains: // - Current position: getX(), getY() // - Historical positions (intermediate points since last frame) override fun onTouchEvent(event: MotionEvent): Boolean { when (event.action) { MotionEvent.ACTION_MOVE -> { // Process historical points for smooth drawing for (i in 0 until event.historySize) { val hx = event.getHistoricalX(i) val hy = event.getHistoricalY(i) val hTime = event.getHistoricalEventTime(i) drawLine(hx, hy) // intermediate points } // Process current point drawLine(event.x, event.y) } } return true }

Multi-Touch: Pointer Index vs Pointer ID

Pointer index — Position in the current MotionEvent's array. Changes between events as fingers are added/removed. Used to access data: getX(pointerIndex).

Pointer ID — Stable identifier for a specific finger. Stays the same for the entire gesture of that finger. Get it via getPointerId(pointerIndex).

Rule: Always track by pointer ID, access data by pointer index. Use findPointerIndex(pointerId) to convert.

Section 9: IPC Mechanisms Compared

Android uses different IPC mechanisms for different scenarios. Understanding why helps you understand architectural decisions throughout the platform.

MechanismFrequencyData SizeLatencySecurityAndroid Use Cases
BinderLow-MediumUp to 1MB per txn~50-100usCaller UID verification, SELinuxSystem service calls, ContentProvider, AIDL services, Intent delivery
Unix SocketHighSmall-Medium~5-20usFile permissions onlyInput events (InputChannel), Zygote fork requests, logd logging
Shared MemoryVery HighLarge (MB+)~0 (no copy)Fd passing via BinderSurfaceFlinger graphics buffers (GraphicBuffer/Gralloc), Ashmem for CursorWindow
PipeMediumSmall~5-10usFile permissionsLooper wake-up (eventfd), parent-child process communication
Why Binder isn't universal: Overhead is too high for high-frequency small data. Each Binder transaction involves: marshalling to Parcel → ioctl into kernel driver → copy to target process → unmarshalling → reply path. At 240Hz touch input, this would add ~50-100us per event plus unpredictable scheduling delays.

Why sockets aren't universal: No built-in caller identity. Binder's killer feature is Binder.getCallingUid() — the kernel driver embeds the caller's UID in the transaction, which cannot be spoofed. Sockets don't provide this, so they can't enforce permission checks.

Why shared memory isn't universal: Complex lifecycle management. Someone has to allocate, map, synchronize access (fences/locks), and free the memory. It's worth the complexity for large data like graphics buffers, but overkill for small messages.
// Real Android IPC examples: // 1. Zygote fork — Unix socket // system_server sends fork request to Zygote via // /dev/socket/zygote (local socket) // Why socket? Zygote predates Binder initialization // 2. SurfaceFlinger buffers — shared memory // App renders to a GraphicBuffer (backed by Gralloc shared mem) // SurfaceFlinger maps the same buffer — zero-copy compositing // Why shared mem? Copying 1080p frames would be insane // 3. logd — Unix socket // Log.d() writes to /dev/socket/logdw // Why socket? High-frequency, fire-and-forget, low latency // 4. ContentProvider cursors — Ashmem (shared memory) // CursorWindow backed by anonymous shared memory // Provider fills it, consumer reads it — no Binder copy // Why shared mem? Cursor data can be large (thousands of rows) // 5. Input events — Unix socket pair (InputChannel) // InputDispatcher writes event to socket // App reads from socket via epoll-backed Looper // Why socket? 240Hz+ events, needs low latency + batching

Section 10: Compose Input — How It Differs

Jetpack Compose does not use the traditional View.onTouchEvent() dispatch chain. But it still ultimately receives events through the same system-level pipeline.

Traditional View System: Jetpack Compose: ───────────────────── ──────────────── ViewRootImpl ViewRootImpl │ │ ▼ ▼ InputStage pipeline InputStage pipeline │ │ ▼ ▼ DecorView.dispatchTouchEvent() DecorView.dispatchTouchEvent() │ │ ▼ ▼ Activity.dispatchTouchEvent() Activity.dispatchTouchEvent() │ │ ▼ ▼ ViewGroup.dispatchTouchEvent() AndroidComposeView.dispatchTouchEvent() │ (recursive through View tree) │ (AndroidComposeView IS a View) ▼ ▼ View.onTouchEvent() LayoutNode hit testing │ (Compose's own node tree) ▼ PointerInputFilter │ ▼ pointerInput { } modifier │ ▼ awaitPointerEventScope { } detectTapGestures { } detectDragGestures { }

AndroidComposeView is a single View subclass that hosts the entire Compose tree. It receives touch events through the normal dispatchTouchEvent() mechanism, then does its own hit testing through the LayoutNode tree — Compose's equivalent of the ViewGroup hierarchy.

// Compose touch handling — coroutine-based API Box( modifier = Modifier .pointerInput(Unit) { awaitPointerEventScope { while (true) { val event = awaitPointerEvent() // event.changes contains all pointer changes event.changes.forEach { change -> val position = change.position // (x, y) val pressed = change.pressed change.consume() // equivalent of returning true } } } } // Or use the high-level gesture detectors: .pointerInput(Unit) { detectTapGestures( onTap = { offset -> /* tap at offset */ }, onLongPress = { offset -> /* long press */ }, onDoubleTap = { offset -> /* double tap */ } ) } ) { /* content */ }
Key differences in Compose input:
1. Coroutine-based: Gesture detection uses suspend functions inside awaitPointerEventScope, making complex gestures readable as sequential code.
2. No onInterceptTouchEvent: Compose uses a different model — PointerEventPass.Initial (parent-first) and PointerEventPass.Final (child-first) give both parent and child a chance without the intercept/steal pattern.
3. consume() replaces return true: Calling change.consume() marks the event as handled, equivalent to returning true from onTouchEvent().
4. Still goes through ViewRootImpl: The system pipeline is identical up to AndroidComposeView. Compose only replaces the in-View-tree dispatch, not the system-level routing.

questions.title: Input Pipeline Stages
questions.description: What is the correct order of major stages that a touch event passes through from hardware to your View?
options: Touch Controller → InputDispatcher → InputReader → Binder → View.onTouchEvent()
Touch Controller → Kernel /dev/input → EventHub → InputReader → InputDispatcher → Unix Socket → ViewRootImpl → View.onTouchEvent()
Touch Controller → InputManagerService (Java) → InputDispatcher → Binder → Activity → View.onTouchEvent()
Touch Controller → SurfaceFlinger → InputReader → InputDispatcher → View.onTouchEvent()
questions.explanation: The complete path is: touch hardware fires IRQ → kernel input driver writes to /dev/input/eventX → EventHub reads raw events → InputReader converts to MotionEvent → InputDispatcher routes to the correct window → Unix socket pair (InputChannel) crosses process boundary → ViewRootImpl receives via Looper/epoll → InputStage pipeline → Activity → ViewGroup tree → View.onTouchEvent(). Note that InputReader and InputDispatcher are separate native threads, and the IPC is a Unix socket, not Binder.
hints: InputReader processes raw events BEFORE InputDispatcher routes them
The IPC between system_server and the app is NOT Binder

questions.title: Why Unix Socket, Not Binder?
questions.description: Explain why Android uses Unix domain sockets (InputChannel) instead of Binder for delivering input events from InputDispatcher to the app process.
questions.answer: Android uses Unix sockets instead of Binder for input events because of latency. Binder transactions involve marshalling data into a Parcel, calling ioctl into the kernel Binder driver, copying data to the target process, unmarshalling, and sending a reply — at least two context switches and two data copies. Unix sockets are direct kernel-buffered pipes: write() copies into a kernel buffer, read() copies out — one context switch, one copy, no marshalling. At 240Hz touch sampling (every ~4.2ms), Binder's ~50-100us overhead per transaction would be unacceptable. The socket is also integrated with the app's Looper via epoll, so events wake the app efficiently without polling.
questions.explanation: The key tradeoff is that sockets don't provide Binder's caller UID verification (security), but input events don't need it — the socket pair is created by the trusted WindowManagerService during window registration, so both endpoints are known. Binder's security features are unnecessary overhead here.
hints: Think about what Binder does under the hood: marshalling, kernel driver, unmarshalling
At 240Hz, how much time budget do you have per event?

questions.title: InputStage Pipeline Order
questions.description: In ViewRootImpl's InputStage pipeline, what is the correct ordering, and why do the IME stages come before the View stages?
options: ViewPostIme → ImeInput → NativePreIme — Views get first priority
NativePreIme → ViewPreIme → ImeInput → EarlyPostIme → NativePostIme → ViewPostIme — IME needs first dibs on key events for text composition
ImeInput → ViewPreIme → ViewPostIme — IME is always first for all events
ViewPreIme → ViewPostIme → ImeInput — Views process, then IME gets leftovers
questions.explanation: The pipeline order is: NativePreIme → ViewPreIme → ImeInput → EarlyPostIme → NativePostIme → ViewPostIme. The IME (soft keyboard) stages come before the main View stage because the Input Method Editor needs first dibs on key events. When you type on a soft keyboard, the key events must reach the IME first so it can compose text (autocomplete, CJK multi-key composition, swipe typing). Touch events skip the IME stages and go directly to ViewPostImeInputStage.
hints: Think about what would happen if the View handled a key event before the soft keyboard saw it
The soft keyboard is an Input Method Editor (IME)

questions.title: onInterceptTouchEvent Behavior
questions.description: A Button is inside a ScrollView. The user presses the Button (ACTION_DOWN) then starts dragging vertically. What happens?
options: Button receives DOWN and all MOVEs — ScrollView never scrolls
Button receives DOWN. When ScrollView detects drag, Button receives ACTION_CANCEL, then ScrollView receives all subsequent MOVE/UP events and scrolls
ScrollView intercepts ACTION_DOWN, Button never receives any events
Both Button and ScrollView receive all events simultaneously
questions.explanation: ScrollView lets ACTION_DOWN pass through to the Button (returns false from onInterceptTouchEvent). The Button highlights itself. Once the finger moves beyond the touch slop threshold, ScrollView's onInterceptTouchEvent() returns true. At this point: (1) the Button receives ACTION_CANCEL, telling it to un-highlight and clean up, (2) all subsequent MOVE and UP events go to ScrollView, which uses them to scroll. This is the intercept-and-steal pattern — the parent lets the child start handling events, then takes over if it decides the gesture is actually for the parent.
hints: ScrollView initially lets the touch pass through to children
What event does a View receive when its parent steals the gesture?

questions.title: ANR Detection Mechanics
questions.description: Where does ANR (Application Not Responding) detection for input events happen, and what triggers it?
options: The Looper in the app process detects that the main thread is blocked and reports to the system
InputDispatcher in the native layer tracks pending events per window — if a window doesn't acknowledge an event within 5 seconds, it reports ANR to InputManagerService
WindowManagerService polls each app every second to check if it's responsive
The kernel watchdog detects that the app process is stuck and sends SIGKILL
questions.explanation: ANR detection for input lives in InputDispatcher (native C++). When InputDispatcher sends an event through the socket, it starts a 5-second timer. The app must send an acknowledgment back through the same socket after processing the event. If the timer expires before the ack arrives, InputDispatcher notifies InputManagerService (Java, in system_server) which triggers the ANR dialog. The key insight is that the app doesn't detect its own ANR — the system side detects it based on the missing acknowledgment.
hints: The detection has to be on the system side — a stuck app can't report its own unresponsiveness
Think about the socket pair: events go in, acknowledgments come back

questions.title: ACTION_CANCEL Semantics
questions.description: What happens in this scenario with a custom View inside a ViewPager?
options: The View works perfectly — ViewPager handles everything

BUG: The View stays highlighted forever. When ViewPager intercepts the horizontal swipe, it sends ACTION_CANCEL, but the View doesn't handle it — tracking remains true and unhighlight() is never called
The View receives ACTION_UP when ViewPager intercepts, so it cleans up normally
ViewPager cannot intercept because the View consumed ACTION_DOWN
questions.explanation: This is a common bug. When ViewPager2 detects a horizontal swipe, its onInterceptTouchEvent() returns true. The custom View receives ACTION_CANCEL instead of ACTION_UP. Since there's no ACTION_CANCEL handler, tracking stays true and unhighlight() is never called — the View is stuck in its highlighted/tracking state. The fix is to always handle ACTION_CANCEL with the same cleanup as ACTION_UP. Every custom touch handler must handle ACTION_CANCEL.
hints: What event does a child receive when a parent intercepts?
It's NOT ACTION_UP — it's a different action type

questions.title: Activity Dispatch Chain Reasoning
questions.description: Why does the touch dispatch chain go DecorView → Activity → Window → DecorView → ViewGroup tree, instead of going directly from DecorView to the ViewGroup tree?
questions.answer: The roundabout path through Activity serves three purposes: (1) Activity.onUserInteraction() is called on every touch event, which resets the screen timeout timer and enables idle detection — this must happen for every touch regardless of which View handles it. (2) Activity can override dispatchTouchEvent() to intercept all touches globally, before any View sees them — useful for lock screens, tutorial overlays, or gesture-based navigation. (3) Activity.onTouchEvent() serves as the last-resort fallback — if no View in the entire tree consumes the event, Activity gets a chance to handle it (e.g., handling touches on the window background).
questions.explanation: The DecorView is the top-level View in the window hierarchy. It could dispatch directly into the ViewGroup tree, but the framework routes through Activity because Activity is the logical owner of the screen — it controls the window lifecycle, manages the UI, and needs hooks for system-level concerns like screen timeout that no individual View should be responsible for.
hints: Think about what system-level behavior must happen on every single touch, no matter which View handles it
What would happen to screen timeout if Activity didn't see every touch?

questions.title: Socket Creation Timing
questions.description: When is the InputChannel (Unix socket pair) for a window created?
options: During Activity.onCreate() when setContentView() is called
During ViewRootImpl.setView() → WindowManagerService.addWindow() — the socket pair is created in system_server and the client fd is returned to the app via Binder
During the first touch event — created lazily when input is first needed
During app process fork from Zygote
questions.explanation: The socket pair is created when the window is added to the system. ViewRootImpl.setView() calls mWindowSession.addToDisplayAsUser(), which is a Binder call to WindowManagerService. WMS calls InputChannel.openInputChannelPair() which does socketpair(AF_UNIX, SOCK_SEQPACKET, 0, fds). The server fd stays in system_server (registered with InputDispatcher), and the client fd is returned to the app process via the Binder reply. The app then registers the client fd with its Looper via Looper.addFd(), which uses epoll underneath.
hints: The window must be registered with WindowManagerService before it can receive input
setContentView() just inflates the layout — it doesn't register the window

questions.title: Missing ACTION_CANCEL Handler
questions.description: This custom seek bar works but has a bug: when the user starts dragging it inside a ScrollView, then ScrollView intercepts the vertical scroll, the thumb stays at the dragged position instead of snapping back. Fix it.

checks.message: Handle ACTION_CANCEL to clean up when the parent intercepts the gesture
Reset the dragging state on cancel

questions.explanation: When a parent (ScrollView) intercepts the gesture via onInterceptTouchEvent() returning true, the child receives ACTION_CANCEL instead of ACTION_UP. Without handling ACTION_CANCEL, the seek bar stays in its dragged position and isDragging remains true. The fix handles ACTION_CANCEL by resetting the drag state and snapping the thumb back to its original position (since the drag was not intentional — the user was scrolling). Every custom touch handler must handle ACTION_CANCEL with appropriate cleanup.
hints: What event does a View receive when its parent intercepts the gesture?
The thumb should go back to where it was before the drag started

questions.title: Compose Input Architecture
questions.description: How does Jetpack Compose receive touch events compared to the View system?
options: Compose bypasses ViewRootImpl entirely and reads from the InputChannel socket directly
Compose uses a completely separate native input pipeline — it doesn't go through InputDispatcher
AndroidComposeView (a single View subclass) receives events via the normal dispatchTouchEvent() chain, then does its own hit testing through the LayoutNode tree
Compose registers its own InputChannel socket pair separately from the View system
questions.explanation: AndroidComposeView is a regular View subclass — it participates in the standard dispatch chain (ViewRootImpl → InputStages → Activity → ViewGroup tree). The entire Compose UI lives inside this single View. Once AndroidComposeView.dispatchTouchEvent() receives the event, it performs its own hit testing through the LayoutNode tree (Compose's equivalent of the ViewGroup hierarchy) and delivers events to pointerInput modifiers. The system-level pipeline is identical; Compose only replaces the in-View-tree dispatch mechanism.
hints: Compose ultimately lives inside a View — AndroidComposeView
The system doesn't know or care whether the window contains Views or Compose
